The downstream LLM should (a) reason about which findings deserve calendar action, (b) consider patterns over single findings, (c) respect coverage_notes and overall_confidence, (d) avoid clinical claims.

behavioral_state_prose:
As of 2026-03-12, recent behavioral observations (overall confidence: high):

Sleep:
  • Sleep onset has been around 00:38 (about 1.5h later than typical 23:07); sustained across the last 3d [pronounced, coverage: high]

Screen & attention:
  • Late-night screen use up 165% vs personal baseline (94min vs typical 35min); sustained across the last 3d [pronounced, coverage: high]

Mobility & location routine:
  • Location routine more restricted than baseline (entropy 1.16 vs 1.77); sustained across the last 3d [pronounced, coverage: high]
  • More time at top-3 frequent places (76% vs typical 60%); sustained across the last 3d [moderate, coverage: high]

Social rhythm & communication:
  • Daily routine timing less regular (SRM 0.63 vs typical 0.80); sustained across the last 3d [moderate, coverage: high]

Coherent patterns worth noting:
  • Sleep onset is drifting later alongside increased late-night screen use — a phone-mediated sleep-delay pattern. Evening calendar density and wind-down timing are plausible leverage points.
    (signals: sleep_onset_hour, late_night_screen_min)
  • Location routine has narrowed and time concentrated at frequent places — a behavioral-withdrawal pattern often associated with lower mood and reduced third-place engagement.
    (signals: mobility_entropy, location_revisit_ratio, social_rhythm_metric)

The LLM scheduler should decide which of the above are worth acting on through calendar adjustments, based on the user's preferences, upcoming commitments, and the coherence of the pattern — not on single-day magnitudes.
